title: How to Evict a Tenant Who Won't Pay Rent: A Step-by-Step Guide for Landlords
excerpt: Dealing with a non-paying tenant is one of the most stressful situations a landlord can face. This guide walks you through every step of the eviction process so you can protect your investment and move forward quickly.
author: AIWholesail Team
tags: how to evict a tenant who wont pay rent
metaDescription: Learn how to evict a tenant who won't pay rent with this step-by-step guide covering notices, court filings, timelines, and costs for landlords.
metaKeywords: how to evict a tenant who wont pay rent, eviction process, non-paying tenant, landlord eviction guide, eviction notice, unlawful detainer, pay or quit notice, eviction timeline, landlord tenant law

sections.content: Every landlord hopes for a smooth, drama-free rental experience. But at some point, you may find yourself asking how to evict a tenant who won't pay rent. Whether your tenant is two weeks late or four months behind, the process can feel overwhelming if you've never done it before. The good news is that eviction follows a clear legal path — and knowing that path puts you in control. This guide breaks down every step so you can act fast, stay legal, and protect your cash flow.
Why You Must Follow the Legal Eviction Process

It's tempting to change the locks or shut off utilities when a tenant stops paying, but those actions are illegal in every U.S. state. Called 'self-help evictions,' they can expose you to lawsuits, fines, and even make it harder to remove the tenant in court. The formal eviction process exists to protect both parties — and skipping steps only drags things out longer. Courts have dismissed eviction cases simply because a landlord served the notice on the wrong day or used the wrong form. Do it right the first time and you'll save weeks of lost rent.
Step 1: Review Your Lease and State Laws

Before you do anything, pull out the signed lease. Check the grace period clause — many leases give tenants 3 to 5 days after the due date before rent is officially late. Next, look up your state's landlord-tenant laws. Eviction rules vary significantly by state and even by city. For example, landlords in Atlanta, GA face different notice requirements than those in Phoenix, AZ or Dallas, TX. Local rules dictate how many days of notice you must give, what the notice must say, and how it must be delivered. Getting this wrong is the number one reason evictions get thrown out of court.

Pro Tip: Bookmark your state's official landlord-tenant statutes online. Many state courts also offer free eviction form templates that are already formatted correctly for local judges. Using official forms dramatically reduces the chance your case gets dismissed on a technicality.
Step 2: Serve a Pay or Quit Notice

The first formal step in the eviction process is serving the tenant a written notice — most commonly called a 'Pay or Quit Notice' or 'Notice to Pay Rent or Vacate.' This document gives the tenant a set number of days to either pay all overdue rent or leave the property. The required notice period varies by state: it's typically 3 days in California and Texas, 5 days in Illinois, and 10 days in some other states. The notice must include the total amount owed, the due date, and instructions for how to pay. You must deliver it properly — either in person, by posting it on the door, or by certified mail, depending on your state's rules.

items: Include the exact amount of rent owed (not late fees, unless your state allows it)
State the deadline clearly — count business days vs. calendar days based on your state law
Deliver the notice using your state's approved method (in-person, posting, certified mail)
Keep a copy and document the date and method of delivery
Do not accept partial rent after serving the notice — it can reset the clock in some states

sections.content: Step 3: File an Eviction Lawsuit (Unlawful Detainer)

If the tenant doesn't pay or leave by the deadline, your next move is to file an eviction lawsuit with your local courthouse. This is typically called an 'Unlawful Detainer' action. Filing fees range from about $50 to $400 depending on your county. Once filed, the court will schedule a hearing — usually within 1 to 4 weeks. A process server or sheriff will deliver the court summons to the tenant, notifying them of the hearing date. At this point, many tenants will choose to leave voluntarily rather than face a court judgment on their record.

'The eviction process isn't just about removing a bad tenant — it's about enforcing a legal contract. Landlords who document everything and follow the law precisely win their cases faster and protect their investment long-term.' — Real Estate Attorney
Step 4: Attend the Court Hearing

Show up to your eviction hearing prepared. Bring your signed lease, copies of all rent payment records, the original Pay or Quit Notice with proof of delivery, and any written communication with the tenant. Judges move quickly through these cases. Be ready to state the facts clearly: the tenant owes X amount, you served proper notice on a specific date, and they did not comply. If the tenant doesn't show up, you'll likely win a default judgment. If they do appear, they may try to argue habitability issues or claim they paid — which is why your documentation matters so much.
Step 5: Get the Writ of Possession

If the judge rules in your favor, the court will issue a 'Writ of Possession' — the legal order that allows you to reclaim your property. The tenant typically has a few more days to vacate voluntarily after this order is issued. If they still refuse to leave, the local sheriff or marshal will physically remove them. This final step can add another 1 to 2 weeks to the timeline. From the date you file to the day you get your keys back, the full eviction process typically takes 3 to 8 weeks depending on your state and local court backlog.
What Does an Eviction Cost?

Beyond lost rent, evictions carry real out-of-pocket costs. Court filing fees run $50 to $400. Process server fees average $75 to $150. If you hire an eviction attorney, add $500 to $1,500 or more. Then factor in cleaning, repairs, and re-renting costs once the tenant is gone. A single eviction can easily cost $3,000 to $5,000 when you add it all up. This is exactly why tenant screening before move-in is so critical — and why tracking your rental income and expenses with a tool like the free [Cash Flow Calculator at aiwholesail.com](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/cash-flow-calculator) helps you understand the true financial impact of a vacancy.
How to Avoid Future Evictions
Screen Tenants More Thoroughly

The best way to handle an eviction is to never need one. Run credit checks, verify income at 2.5 to 3 times the monthly rent, call previous landlords, and check criminal background. Most evictions can be traced back to poor tenant screening. If you're newer to landlording, the [Real Estate Investing for Beginners guide at aiwholesail.com](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/real-estate-investing-for-beginners) covers the fundamentals of building a rental portfolio the right way from the start.
Build Cash Reserves Into Your Investment Strategy

Smart investors always plan for vacancy. Whether you're running a single rental or scaling a portfolio using strategies like those covered in the [BRRRR Method guide at aiwholesail.com](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/brrrr-method-explained), having 3 to 6 months of expenses in reserve means a non-paying tenant doesn't threaten your financial stability. Markets like [Atlanta](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/atlanta-ga), [Dallas](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/dallas-tx), and [Phoenix](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/phoenix-az) all have strong rental demand — but even in hot markets, a single bad tenant can derail an otherwise profitable property.

items: Always use a written, state-compliant lease — never go month-to-month without one
Collect a security deposit equal to at least one month's rent
Respond quickly to maintenance requests to avoid habitability defenses in court
Set up automatic rent payment reminders or online payment portals
Document the property's condition with photos at move-in and move-out

sections.content: Pro Tip: Consider hiring an eviction attorney for your first case even if it seems straightforward. Watching an experienced attorney handle the paperwork and hearing once will teach you the process thoroughly — and many offer flat-fee eviction services under $500.
Key Eviction Timeline at a Glance

items: Day 1: Rent is officially late after the grace period ends
Day 1–3: Serve the Pay or Quit Notice (start counting the notice period)
Day 3–10: Notice period expires with no payment or move-out
Day 10–14: File Unlawful Detainer with the courthouse
Day 14–35: Court hearing scheduled and held
Day 35–50: Writ of Possession issued and enforced by sheriff if needed
Day 50+: Property is vacant and ready to re-rent
